metadata.from: Bob Hanifen (Pvt. Robert Hanifen, based on return address on envelope)
metadata.to: Hanifen Body & Paint Co. (James Edward Hanifen Sr., Bob's father), Des Moines, Iowa
metadata.location_sent_to: Hanifen Body & Paint Co., 904 Geo Way [George Way?], Des Moines, Iowa
metadata.postmark: Wichita Falls, Texas, Jun 2, 12:30 PM, 1946 [though letter says June 1 — postmark year may be 1945 misread as 1946]
metadata.envelope_notes: Return address: Pvt. Bob Hanifen 17170630, Sqd. I-3706 ABO, AAF-B-T-U-BKSSOZ, Sheppard Field, Texas. Air Mail stamp 6 cents. Postage Due 2 cents stamp affixed. 'POSTAGE DUE, 2 CENTS' rubber stamp in purple ink. Number '107' written in pencil on front.
metadata.mood_tone: Upbeat and chatty; Bob describes a busy, somewhat exhausting military training schedule with humor and good spirits, especially recounting the surprise gas attack on Memorial Day morning.
metadata.legibility_notes: Handwriting is generally readable but some words are difficult due to cursive style and bleed-through from the other side of the page. A few words on page 3 (e.g., 'Berle fields' and the squadron/classification references) are partially illegible. The postmark year is difficult to read — appears to say 1946 but letter content and context suggest 1945.
metadata.historical_context: Bob is in Army Air Forces basic training at Sheppard Field, Texas, likely mid-1945 (WWII still ongoing or just ended). References to gas mask drills, combat courses, bobby traps [booby traps], and classification/orders reflect wartime military training. Memorial Day gas attack drill reflects realistic wartime training exercises. Other cadets (30 yr. or over) receiving 15-day delay en route likely reflects demobilization or reassignment processing near or after V-E Day (May 8, 1945).

pages.transcription:
Fri. 6:30 PM
June, 1

Dear Dad
    I just received your combination letter from you and Edith. It was a very nice letter. The days are really flying around here. I bet that parade you were in was pretty rough. That a pretty long haul. By the way I am going to tell you what we did Memorial Day. I was rudely awakely [sic] at 6:30 the yelling of a Dozen guys, yelling gas attack, in a flash I was up. Took a quick breath and turn around to get my mask. In a flash I had it on, clear it and starting to breath again I could see the gas coming in through the windows. Every-body in the barracks

acted just as fast. The whole camp was gas at 8:00 with HCL smoke and tear gas. Boy the stuff was everywhere. I finished dressing and by the time chow was rounded the all clear was given. We than went to a couple of classes and than to the Combat Courses, to set and disarmed bobby traps [booby traps]. P.T. and than chow. Drill and B.T. in the afternoon again and a few lectures. I got back to the barracks and somebody said it was Memorial Day. Hell I didn't even know it. Thur. was pay day. The whole got a holiday from near, We whnt [sic] down to the Squadron at 7:30 and finally got pay at 1:30 in the afternoon a gang of us guys than went

swimming had a swell time, eat a good chicken fried steak, seen a show and started back. When we got to the gate. The M.P. said for all Cadet to report immediately to their Squad. for reclassification. It was 11:00 than. at 12:30 they came in and got 12 A.M. gunner and told them they wre [sic] leaving for Berle [Barle? Bartle?] fields, I sues [guess?]. I never got no order yet, so it means I continue on till I do. I should get my orders in a couple of days. Had a rough day today. 2 hours of movie P.T. and another lecture. In the afternoon B.T. again and Drill and a few lectures. You marched about 10 miles a day going to and from lectures and P.T. We really got Drill

to. The Sarge over us really gave us a going over. Tomorrow. We have a parade and big inspection in the afternoon regular basic. I am going to send home 30 dollars as soon as I have time to get to the Post office. It rained for about 10 min. this morning and really helped to keep the dust down. Well Dad goodby for now and I will write again soon. My orders should be here in a couple of days. All the old guys, 30 yr. or over, get a 15 day delay in route. Isn't that a boo loo [boo-hoo?]. Well goodby for now
    Your loving Son
        Bob

full_transcription:
Fri. 6:30 PM
June, 1

Dear Dad
    I just received your combination letter from you and Edith. It was a very nice letter. The days are really flying around here. I bet that parade you were in was pretty rough. That a pretty long haul. By the way I am going to tell you what we did Memorial Day. I was rudely awakely [sic] at 6:30 the yelling of a Dozen guys, yelling gas attack, in a flash I was up. Took a quick breath and turn around to get my mask. In a flash I had it on, clear it and starting to breath again I could see the gas coming in through the windows. Every-body in the barracks acted just as fast. The whole camp was gas at 8:00 with HCL smoke and tear gas. Boy the stuff was everywhere. I finished dressing and by the time chow was rounded the all clear was given. We than went to a couple of classes and than to the Combat Courses, to set and disarmed bobby traps [booby traps]. P.T. and than chow. Drill and B.T. in the afternoon again and a few lectures. I got back to the barracks and somebody said it was Memorial Day. Hell I didn't even know it. Thur. was pay day. The whole got a holiday from near, We whnt [sic] down to the Squadron at 7:30 and finally got pay at 1:30 in the afternoon a gang of us guys than went swimming had a swell time, eat a good chicken fried steak, seen a show and started back. When we got to the gate. The M.P. said for all Cadet to report immediately to their Squad. for reclassification. It was 11:00 than. at 12:30 they came in and got 12 A.M. gunner and told them they wre [sic] leaving for Berle [Barle? Bartle?] fields, I sues [guess?]. I never got no order yet, so it means I continue on till I do. I should get my orders in a couple of days. Had a rough day today. 2 hours of movie P.T. and another lecture. In the afternoon B.T. again and Drill and a few lectures. You marched about 10 miles a day going to and from lectures and P.T. We really got Drill to. The Sarge over us really gave us a going over. Tomorrow. We have a parade and big inspection in the afternoon regular basic. I am going to send home 30 dollars as soon as I have time to get to the Post office. It rained for about 10 min. this morning and really helped to keep the dust down. Well Dad goodby for now and I will write again soon. My orders should be here in a couple of days. All the old guys, 30 yr. or over, get a 15 day delay in route. Isn't that a boo loo [boo-hoo?]. Well goodby for now
    Your loving Son
        Bob
summary: Bob Hanifen writes to his father from Sheppard Field, Texas on June 1 (likely 1945), describing his busy and exhausting days of military training including PT, drill, lectures, and combat course exercises. He vividly recounts a surprise gas attack drill on Memorial Day morning that woke the entire barracks, and describes how the holiday was otherwise spent on pay day activities including swimming and a chicken fried steak dinner. Bob notes he is awaiting his orders for reassignment, expects them within a couple of days, and plans to send $30 home as soon as he can get to the post office.
